text: Do political parties care about voter opinions? And more broadly, do they care about citizens? Most work in political science treats this as a prima facie empirically supported statement. In this paper we challenge this conventional wisdom. Moving beyond the many familiar metrics documenting that parties are not typically in high degree of positional congruence with citizens, we address the far more important question of whether parties are more often inclined to prioritize those issues that citizens prioritize—whether an agreement exists between citizens’ issue priorities and party’s policy priorities. We provide evidence indicating generally that such an agreement does exist and that parties tend to follow voter preferences in their election manifestos; however our investigation shows that this relation is not constant for all parties and, therefore, not automatic or unconditional. Rather than being a general principle of political life, party-voter congruence by issues appears contingent on the type of party. Large parties (including ruling parties and main opposition parties, that we view as core parties in multiparty systems) on average tend to be more responsive to voters issues, with ruling parties being the least responsive. In the meantime, niche parties do not tend on average to be more responsive to voters; however they tend to be more responsive to the priorities of voters that fall in their core policy domain, which we call an “owned” policy domain. Our main finding is derived from a unique combination of data from the Manifesto Project with the voter priority data available for the seven countries in the Comparative Study of Electoral Systems and other national election studies. We use 63 elections between 1972 and 2011 covering 18 European countries to empirically test the political science and the social science theories and hypotheses underlying our alternative explanation of the workings of political representation, while providing significant contributions to the empirical knowledge of the functioning of democracy.
